title: CHAPTER 5 —
THE FIVE SUPERVISORY CAPACITIES
segments.body_html:
Before I take them one at a time, here is the whole list, because the list is the argument:

Chapter 5 — The Five Supervisory Capacit — Problem Formulation — deciding what the build is before Claude sees it. Claude optimizes within the frame you give it. I

beats.text: Before I take them one at a time, here is the whole list, because the list is the argument: Problem Formulation , deciding what the build is before Claude sees it. Claude optimizes within the frame you give it. If the frame is wrong, the output is wrong, elegantly.

segments.label: 01 / 11 · HOOK

title: PROBLEM FORMULATION
segments.body_html: →Claude produces output whether you've asked them or not→

beats.text: Seth's NPC classifier is a textbook failure that almost shipped. The frame he gave Claude was: write a function that scores a state tag against a category name. Claude returned a function that scores a state tag against a category name. The output is excellent inside that frame. Outside that frame , in the world where the event log says haunting when the category is hunting, where the error mode is semantic substitution rather than spelling drift , the frame is the wrong question.

segments.label: 02 / 11 · PROBLEM FORMULATION

title: TOOL ORCHESTRATION
segments.body_html: →→→

beats.text: If decides what the build is, decides how to assemble it from Claude's actions. Claude Code is not one tool. You can ask it to plan, to write, to read, to refactor, to run tests, to run scripts, to search, to commit. Each of those is a different task type with a different failure profile. A planning prompt produces a list; if the list is wrong, you waste a build. A writing prompt produces code; if the code is plausible-but-wrong, you waste a debug.

segments.label: 03 / 11 · TOOL ORCHESTRATION

title: PLAUSIBILITY AUDITING
segments.body_html: Plausibility Auditing→→→→

beats.text: The move Seth made when he typed hunting and haunting , before any formal verification, before the test runner confirmed anything , is Plausibility Auditing. It is the capacity to hear the wrong note before the proof is ready. Michael Polanyi, who was a physical chemist before he became a philosopher, built a career on a single sentence: we can know more than we can tell (Polanyi, 1966).

segments.label: 04 / 11 · PLAUSIBILITY AUDITING

title: INTERPRETIVE JUDGMENT
segments.body_html:
92%
accuracy on a fresh test set of 200 labeled NPC state tags
→
92%
is a number
→
8%
errors are random spelling drift or systematic semantic subs

beats.text: Suppose Seth's new classifier reports 92 percent accuracy on a fresh test set of 200 labeled NPC state tags. On its own: not enough to make any decision. 92 percent is a number. It does not say what the class distribution was. It does not say whether the 8 percent errors are random spelling drift or systematic semantic substitution. It does not say whether 92 percent is good for this use case, given this student population, on this deadline. The number is a measurement.

segments.label: 05 / 11 · INTERPRETIVE JUDGMENT

title: EXECUTIVE INTEGRATION
segments.body_html:
There is one more, and it catches the failure that hurts most when it gets through.

Executive Integration — Builds drift. A long Claude session accumulates small decisions, small concessions, small let's just for nows, and the a

beats.text: There is one more, and it catches the failure that hurts most when it gets through. Builds drift. A long Claude session accumulates small decisions, small concessions, small let's just for nows, and the accumulated drift between where you started and where you are at minute ninety is rarely visible in any single step. Each individual move is locally reasonable. Each individual move is, in isolation, a sensible answer to the immediate problem.

segments.label: 06 / 11 · EXECUTIVE INTEGRATION

title: SETH'S BUILD,
LABELED.
segments.body_html:
85%
accuracy, all pass
→
85%
means for *this* use case — short state tags in a horror gam

beats.text: Here is the full arc of Seth's forty minutes, with every capacity named where it appeared. He starts before he opens Claude. He spends two minutes writing down what correct means for his classifier: spelling drift is acceptable, semantic substitution is not, ambiguous tags go to a review queue rather than getting auto-rolled into a category. He writes this as a comment block in an empty file. That is . The frame is on the page in his words before Claude sees the task.

segments.label: 07 / 11 · SETH'S BUILD, LABELED

title: 🕰️ AI
WAYBACK MACHINE.
segments.body_html: Barbara McClintock→H-LAM/T system→PA, PF, TO, IJ, EI

beats.text: Barbara McClintock is the person who, in 1962, wrote the report that put the structural argument behind what this chapter is saying. Augmenting Human Intellect: A Conceptual Framework (AFOSR-3223, SRI Project 3578) was a sixty-six-page document prepared for the Air Force Office of Scientific Research, written before Engelbart had invented the mouse, before the 1968 "Mother of All Demos," before most of the computing infrastructure we now take for granted existed.

segments.label: 08 / 11 · 🕰️ AI WAYBACK MACHINE
